speaker: Sam Schrager
words: This interview with Glenn Gilder and Agnes Clark Gilder was recorded at their home near Spring Valley on July 29th, 1975.

speaker: Glen or Agnes or Richard Gilder
words: Lived and were around there, you couldn't help but know him. Laird and Humiston.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you remember Laird as being a sort of aristocratic kind of a man?

speaker: Glen or Agnes or Richard Gilder
words: Oh,

speaker: Glen or Agnes or Richard Gilder
words: I think he walked with his shoulders back alright.

speaker: Glen or Agnes or Richard Gilder
words: I've seen men with less that had more swelled hair. No, he wasn't overbearing or anything. He knew you. No matter where they met you, no matter how you were dressed. Then a little bit later they had another one, Jack Irwin. Same way.

speaker: Glen or Agnes or Richard Gilder
words: O 'Connell and some of them birds.

speaker: Glen or Agnes or Richard Gilder
words: They wanted you to know that they would love us. Big shot. A lot of them. There'd been a string of them since those two or three. Oh, yeah. Laird and Irwin. Irwin didn't stay too long. Then O 'Connell came and he stayed about 10 or 12 years. Then all of them had been string of them since then. I don't know who they are. I don't know. I guess so. Yeah, something like that. I liked Irwin the best of all of them.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was he like and what made him friendly?

speaker: Glen or Agnes or Richard Gilder
words: Kind of a rough shape now. Just a common old lumberjack. We're in a big position but it didn't go over your head.

speaker: Glen or Agnes or Richard Gilder
words: It's not uncommon, there never has been, for a man to get up with a little position to get a testicle and swelled up about it. Some of them were, some of them weren't.

speaker: Glen or Agnes or Richard Gilder
words: I've heard that a number of those people on Knob Hill were not all that as well off maybe as they would want people to think. Oh, they had, well, let's see. There was Laird and Humiston and Sigerson. And then around on the side was Eric Mattson and George Anderson. Mattson was an assistant superintendent and George Anderson was a banker. And Harts Hansen lived up there. He worked in the bank. All the ills kind of supposed to be upper crust. Just a damn nice fella up there.

speaker: Glen or Agnes or Richard Gilder
words: Well, in general one of them was pretty good people.

speaker: Glen or Agnes or Richard Gilder
words: I got awful well acquainted with them because we had dairy over there and I called it them houses day after day after day with their milk.

speaker: Glen or Agnes or Richard Gilder
words: I wondered whether they'd have much to do with the social life of the other ordinary people. They kind of separated. Yeah, they did.

speaker: Glen or Agnes or Richard Gilder
words: They seldom went to the public dances down in the gym. Once in a while there would be some of them in there but not too many. They kind of had their own clique and they stayed in that clique. He's talking about Knob Hill.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Knob Hill? Is that what they used to call it?

speaker: Glen or Agnes or Richard Gilder
words: Well, they called it everything.

speaker: Glen or Agnes or Richard Gilder
words: Well, I couldn't see anything different from people lived on that hill and the people that lived on the other one across the road.

speaker: Glen or Agnes or Richard Gilder
words: That's Shelton Andrews outfit. They lived right back to the church and they were right in between. Oh, that's okay. Sounds good now that you say that. No, no. It wasn't anything like that. He is still around I think.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So?

speaker: Glen or Agnes or Richard Gilder
words: No, he did. But anyway, I think he was the biggest snob in the whole damn town is where my opinion of him.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was he like?

speaker: Glen or Agnes or Richard Gilder
words: He was the master mechanic and he got out of school and just because he was Shelton Andrews, Larry put him right in top of that machine shop. Under filled thighs of course but...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Will it take some relation to Larry's?

speaker: Glen or Agnes or Richard Gilder
words: I don't think so.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was it just his manner?

speaker: Glen or Agnes or Richard Gilder
words: I think so.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Just his bearing or something?

speaker: Glen or Agnes or Richard Gilder
words: Yeah.

speaker: Glen or Agnes or Richard Gilder
words: I was sent into the shop over there with a job to be done at the forge and Ollie Nargad was the head blacksmith. He was a very close friend of mine. So Shelton come walking in, I was standing there by the forge and Ollie was working and we were talking. And Shelton told me that the only people allowed in here were to be working and turned around and walked away. Well, I'd been sent in there by one of the other bosses so the thing for me to do is stay there and I did. Pretty soon he come back. I told you that they stayed in here. The people that are in here are supposed to be working. Well, I don't know whether I'm working or not but said Brock sent me over here with a job to be done and I expect I probably better get it done. He turned and walked away again. That was the last scene of him but he was going to, you know, just only thought he had. He didn't give a damn whether I was there or not. He wanted to throw his voice and I let him throw it.

speaker: Glen or Agnes or Richard Gilder
words: But we were close to the same age. We knew each other while we were going to school and before we were married around at dances and things. But we never clicked.

speaker: Glen or Agnes or Richard Gilder
words: So it really went to his head when he got a little power. Well, when he went to Lewiston from over there, he went down the machine shop in Lewiston.

speaker: Glen or Agnes or Richard Gilder
words: Johnny Johnson owned this place up here where Milton lives now or has. Milton was under Scheldt and pretty soon he came up the line and went over Scheldt.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And I knew it and one day I asked Johnny, how did you get along with Scheldt, Johnny?

speaker: Glen or Agnes or Richard Gilder
words: Real good. He said that nobody could get along that SOB.

speaker: Glen or Agnes or Richard Gilder
words: I think he probably couldn't have any friends. I think he had a lot of friends that scratched his elbow just for the position he held and assumed that he held.

speaker: Glen or Agnes or Richard Gilder
words: I think there was a lot of that going on. More of that went on in Potlatch than any place I ever lived.

speaker: Glen or Agnes or Richard Gilder
words: You think that's because...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What were you going to say?

speaker: Glen or Agnes or Richard Gilder
words: Well, I think I said too much already.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Have you got any matches?

speaker: Glen or Agnes or Richard Gilder
words: Yeah.

speaker: Glen or Agnes or Richard Gilder
words: Why don't you click that thing off? Is that damn thing on?

speaker: Glen or Agnes or Richard Gilder
words: Holy jeez.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How are you going to get the story out of those things? Have you clicked it off?

speaker: Glen or Agnes or Richard Gilder
words: I think so, yeah. Potlatch was just a cross cut of all these little industrial towns. At that time they had to have a separation in society and that's the way they did it. It's a laboring man and an officer on that.

speaker: Glen or Agnes or Richard Gilder
words: There's some kindling there on the side. But you know, I had a funny way of judging that proposition. I was selling milk in there and I sold milk on La Biel and on all the rest of the hills. Those fellows in the higher brackets that were always employed, they always had money and they were good pay and all that. While over on the other hill, they weren't always employed. They'd be laid off for one reason or another.

speaker: Glen or Agnes or Richard Gilder
words: A week after they were laid off, they were broke. But even though there was a few people up on Knob Hill, they wouldn't have to be laid off. If you'd stole me off from a few days to a month on that milk bill, it was only from $3 to $6 a month. It was $9.

speaker: Glen or Agnes or Richard Gilder
words: $0 .25 a quart, wasn't it then?

speaker: Glen or Agnes or Richard Gilder
words: No, $12 .50. $0 .08 a quart of the prize bill. That wouldn't add up to too much. No, no. Take a quart or two. Like Eric Matheson had quite a family, he had, I think he took three quarts. But it would amaze you that people up there in that higher bracket, that they were paid to sell you off. For no reason at all, you could see, they'd have to stall you off for 10, 15, 20 days to make your payments.

speaker: Glen or Agnes or Richard Gilder
words: Well, I had people over on the other hill, you know, the common labouring class?

speaker: Glen or Agnes or Richard Gilder
words: And I know three or four of them that were laid off clear through that depression and never missed a dog van payment. So I figured the best man was over on the other hill. In my estimation, that's where most capable men were, on the other hill. Because it would take capability to judge finances to that extent. Carl Haag never missed a payment.

speaker: Glen or Agnes or Richard Gilder
words: How many children do they have? Eight?

speaker: Glen or Agnes or Richard Gilder
words: I don't remember, six or eight. Quite a number for those. Quite a bunch of them.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: These Nob Hill people still had jobs for Potlatch during that time?

speaker: Glen or Agnes or Richard Gilder
words: Oh yeah, the Nob Hill bunch were, yes, they were studied and employed because they were railroad gambling, his crew, Marshing and Elsie, and all those people. They were employed every doggone day.

speaker: Glen or Agnes or Richard Gilder
words: While the men, the mill crew, see, some mills shut down. Most of them were just in desperate fix, in a few days. But there was just quite a few of them, a surprising bunch,

speaker: Glen or Agnes or Richard Gilder
words: that weren't ever hurt too bad.

speaker: Glen or Agnes or Richard Gilder
words: It doesn't seem like a lot of those people could, from what you're saying too, could get really got ahead very much by the time they were done with what they had to spend their wages on. It sounds like they just got by for years. Well, most of them, that's true. But, this Ole Nygaard, he told me this story several times. He came over from the old country and he landed in Potlatch practically broke. I think he had less than five dollars.

speaker: Glen or Agnes or Richard Gilder
words: So, he worked a week or two and made a draw and he bought an old secondhand cook stove and rented a house and had a secondhand cook stove and enough dishes and stuff to cook his own meals. And, they batched.

speaker: Glen or Agnes or Richard Gilder
words: He waited then until he got another payday and he bought some more blankets that he needed and some more dishes that he needed and got his bills all squared away. But, he didn't have a bed to sleep on. He didn't have a table nor a chair in the house. He had some bedding, but he slept on the floor. And, he says, I just saved every cent I could and after a while, he says, I had enough money saved up to send to the old country for my wife and kids. And, he says, they came and he said, I had to get a few more dishes and a little more bedding. But, he says, we were two months before we had enough money to get tables and chairs and bedsteads. He slept on the floor and ate all the boxes. This bat's getting that something out. And, when Ollie wound up, he was wealthy. He owned one of the nicest places between Palouse and Potlatch.

speaker: Glen or Agnes or Richard Gilder
words: Just a hell of a good man.

speaker: Glen or Agnes or Richard Gilder
words: Was it all savings from the working and the... He didn't have anything else. He used just what he saved out of his wages until he bought the place and then he... Of course, he had, after he got it paid for, well then he had a very good income from his place, but it took him... I think he told me, the last time I talked to him, he told me it took him 28 years to pay for that place. But, he says, by the guy, he says, no, she's all paid for. Do you think...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did he have a better job or did he have the same kind of job?

speaker: Glen or Agnes or Richard Gilder
words: Well, he was a blacksmith. A forged blacksmith. And, he was absolutely an artist. Just anything you could throw in front of him. If there was a broken iron in that mill, he could fix it. Yes. And, that's a fact.

speaker: Glen or Agnes or Richard Gilder
words: They had artists in those days. They had a man that used to fix them belts. The belts were all leather.

speaker: Glen or Agnes or Richard Gilder
words: And, hell, they were from 12 feet wide down to an inch and a half.

speaker: Glen or Agnes or Richard Gilder
words: Lee Larimore was a belt man and if they'd break a belt, he had nothing to slip on. And, he had a bunch of adjustable wheels that he'd put just the width that he wanted them. Well, most belts, but not all of them. And, then he'd get in there with a plane and he'd plane that leather down and he'd insert a patch of that leather in there with glue and this and that. And, you couldn't hardly tell where it was patched. It was just that good. You know, the big belts that run that whole sawmill was 8 or 10 or 12 feet wide. I don't remember. But, it was wide. And, they broke that one day and he didn't have any place to splice it, but he was accepting right on the wheel. And, they made a couple of clamps and put on there and bolted them together, pulled that belt back to where he wanted it. And, then just started in and he spliced that belt. So, it helped me. You couldn't hardly tell where he'd been working on it. Just that smooth.

speaker: Glen or Agnes or Richard Gilder
words: He'd leave one piece of leather a little bit thick. The next one under it, he'd thin it down just a certain amount and then he'd make it kind of a wedge.

speaker: Glen or Agnes or Richard Gilder
words: And, there were 3 or 4 plies and no 2 plies had a joint in the same place. It'd be about that far apart.

speaker: Glen or Agnes or Richard Gilder
words: And, each splice I built one evening when I was on shift, on an afternoon shift and I spent half my shift watching him.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did guys like him and the blacksmith who were really highly skilled in their field, did they get much better paid than others?

speaker: Glen or Agnes or Richard Gilder
words: I think so. I think they did.

speaker: Glen or Agnes or Richard Gilder
words: But, they'd just be peanuts to what they would get today. And, those skills don't exist. You couldn't find a man in the state of Idaho who could splice a belt. I'll bet you.

speaker: Glen or Agnes or Richard Gilder
words: I don't think you could find a man in the state of Idaho that could make a tool as quick and it would stand up as good as all we could. Ed Compton, Stover Douglas, Mike Murphy over their mail rights.

speaker: Glen or Agnes or Richard Gilder
words: They had a big job on their hands. That's a big mill and they was roughing it. But, they kept running. If they had to work all night they'd work, but they got her. Malcolm Greer was one of the crew. Ed Compton, Stover Douglas, Mike Murphy, Guy Puckett.

speaker: Glen or Agnes or Richard Gilder
words: If there's any more I've forgotten them, but by darn if she broke down they might miss her supper and breakfast, but they'd get her together.

speaker: Glen or Agnes or Richard Gilder
words: They grew up in Sawmills and they grew up under men that had more adverse conditions in Sawmills than they had and they really knew their biddiness.

speaker: Glen or Agnes or Richard Gilder
words: They'd come east or west with the company and died right there on the job in town anyway.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were they a set crew, the maintenance crew?

speaker: Glen or Agnes or Richard Gilder
words: That was their job.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was most of that work usually done at night?

speaker: Glen or Agnes or Richard Gilder
words: Yes. Well, if they had to shut a machine down and there was room to work around it, well then they could do it and the rest of the mill running. But, if a machine couldn't be worked on while the mill was running, if it wasn't something that shut the saws and the edger and so forth down, they'd just keep right on going and then when the whistle blew these guys would pitch into it.

speaker: Glen or Agnes or Richard Gilder
words: You know, you hear men whine and growl about their jobs now being such a headache and such a hard job and all that. I've known them meant to be called out at midnight. Something would break and it might be thirty below zero. They didn't kick. They'd come down there just as good -natured as if they'd just go into a party, go to work, just fine. You never hear one of them kick.

speaker: Glen or Agnes or Richard Gilder
words: I sort of wonder about these hours and the wages in those days. Art Sundberg was telling me it was a ten hour day, no overtime, and you could expect on a lot of jobs fairly often to work an extra two and a half hours at the mill. That's right. For some reason it's just all we needed and you were expected to do it. No excuse.

speaker: Glen or Agnes or Richard Gilder
words: Take those guys that were on shift and somebody on one side or the other would be sick or couldn't come to work or didn't come to work. If you were on the shift and he was next, no questions asked, you stayed right there the next eight hours, sixteen hours straight through. If you were lucky, your wife or somebody brought you lunch, that was fine, but if they didn't, that was fine too. Nobody give a damn.

speaker: Glen or Agnes or Richard Gilder
words: That seems unbelievable as a fact.

speaker: Glen or Agnes or Richard Gilder
words: You guys didn't think twice about it yourselves.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you feel that it was a pretty tough hoe then or did it just seem like the natural things ought to be done?

speaker: Glen or Agnes or Richard Gilder
words: It is something that ought to be done and it just felt you were lucky to do it, so do it and keep quiet about it. You just got paid at the same rate per hour for sixteen hours as if you had just put in eight hours. No difference. You just got sixteen hours. We liked that, same as they like overtime now.

speaker: Glen or Agnes or Richard Gilder
words: In a way, we liked it financially, but lots of them jobs were damn hard work. You were pretty damn tired when night come after sixteen hours of it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What were you doing in the mill when you started working there?

speaker: Glen or Agnes or Richard Gilder
words: Firing the boilers.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When was it that you started?

speaker: Glen or Agnes or Richard Gilder
words: 1928. Started in the mill. I fired for two or three years and then got in a fight and got promoted. Yeah, he told me about that. That was a good story.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Glenn, what was the story you told me about the doctor telling you that you were going to stay there? What was that story?

speaker: Glen or Agnes or Richard Gilder
words: He asked me what I was doing. I told him I was working in the mill.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Oh, you got your wings clipped, huh?

speaker: Glen or Agnes or Richard Gilder
words: I said, I'm not sure.

speaker: Glen or Agnes or Richard Gilder
words: He figured if they ever started working in the mill, they'd never leave it. And most of them didn't either.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What do you think it was that caused so much brown nosing in a place like Potlatch?

speaker: Glen or Agnes or Richard Gilder
words: Well, actually, it wasn't any worse at Potlatch than it was any place else. There's a class of people even today that'll do that, try to promote themselves.

speaker: Glen or Agnes or Richard Gilder
words: If they have to demote somebody else, that's all right. But I heard a preacher say one time that that's a sin and I wouldn't be surprised if maybe it is. I don't know.

speaker: Glen or Agnes or Richard Gilder
words: You take a hundred men and a crew and you're just going to find it then or now or 50 years before or 50 years afterward.

speaker: Glen or Agnes or Richard Gilder
words: They never gain anything by it?

speaker: Glen or Agnes or Richard Gilder
words: Never.

speaker: Glen or Agnes or Richard Gilder
words: That dust is good.

speaker: Glen or Agnes or Richard Gilder
words: Oh, boys like Art can tell you that saw mill story better than I could because I didn't stay there ten years because my wings weren't clipped. And Art spent his life there. I don't blame him. Roy Berry is another, Earl Compton is another, Tom Donahue is another. And just go on and on. They spent their life there. I don't think they did wrong. You know, I was thinking about it the other day. It's just a cog in those wheels. So many hundreds of people are involved in getting out these products. And other people are profiting quite a bit from it. The stockholders, the shareholders. The difference between that and then the small farmer who is the kind of guy that broke the frontier and pioneered the country. They seem like such a different way of life to me. Like the old way that made the West and then this new way which sort of dominates America. It seemed like a real, really big difference when I think about it. And the kind of way the whole business is set up. You're a farmer and you're just doing it for yourself. One man operation. And if you're working for the mill, you're part of a big corporation. You are a cog. Well,

speaker: Glen or Agnes or Richard Gilder
words: you know, there is, there always has been and always will be people that can fit in and be a crew and under a boss. And they're satisfied there. And it isn't any reflection on their judgment or anything. It's what they like to do. Now, they don't have any responsibility excepting just this little nick that they're in. And then you find guys like myself that don't like to work for somebody else and don't like to be told what to do. And if I earn a dollar, I don't want to split it 70 to 30.

speaker: Glen or Agnes or Richard Gilder
words: Or 90 -10. That's the reason people like me get away from it. And that's the reason that the other guys, if they're satisfied, they stay there. Now, chances are good that they had a darn side easier life, less worry and less bother, or less worry and less concern than I had. Because I had to fight my way through it money -wise, get the work done, get equipment to do the work with, and all those things. They didn't have that worry. They didn't want it. But I did. I wouldn't fight my own. And that's the reason I got away from it.

speaker: Glen or Agnes or Richard Gilder
words: Those guys are retired. They've got good problems all solved. A little savings and a pretty good pension. And they're fine. They're just okay. And I'm just in the same boat, that's all. After it's all wound up, I'm just in the same boat. I'm close to the mountain, fine, doing alright. That's what I worked for, that's what I had planned, and I made it. Of course the thing that I think goes along with that, which I would think would be tougher, I wonder how people would deal with it. You're in a place like Potlatch where you've got all the brass up on Nob Hill. There's such a hierarchy, such a system where you've got your place and then these other guys are above you. And then Laird sits there on top of the whole heap. And you have to live with it. And you seem very set up in a pattern that way. A guy who works for himself isn't... Well, he's not in that hierarchy, but at the same time, don't think he don't have problems. But those guys had a good life and an easy life. And they're doing fine now.

speaker: Glen or Agnes or Richard Gilder
words: After all, what else is there?

speaker: Glen or Agnes or Richard Gilder
words: I was going to ask you, Glenn, about when you started working out when you were a kid.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What did you do when you started working out?

speaker: Glen or Agnes or Richard Gilder
words: Anything I could get. I generally could get a job driving somebody's horses, doing something. That just comes as natural to me as breathing.

speaker: Glen or Agnes or Richard Gilder
words: I generally followed that. Then when I went into mill, I worked around the boilers for a few years and fired and tended water and watched what was going on. And when I left there, then I went out running an engine on a small mill.

speaker: Glen or Agnes or Richard Gilder
words: And I run that until steam went out of style and that was it. Then I learned to edge and finished out a few years farming and saw mill nights or saw mill day times and farm nights.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Farm nights?

speaker: Glen or Agnes or Richard Gilder
words: Yeah, and Sundays.

speaker: Glen or Agnes or Richard Gilder
words: Quite a few years. And I and the boys did all the work here on the place, kept it right up to snuff.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Put a headlight on the horse team?

speaker: Glen or Agnes or Richard Gilder
words: No. No, it didn't seem like we had to. We didn't need a headlight until we got a tractor. We was always right up. We'd keep right up. And then after we got a tractor, of course we had more land by that time and more to do.

speaker: Glen or Agnes or Richard Gilder
words: I used to figure to come home Friday night and run until 11 or 12 o 'clock with the tractor. And then come in and sleep and go back out probably at 8 o 'clock and run until 11 or 12 again.

speaker: Glen or Agnes or Richard Gilder
words: Then on Sunday,

speaker: Glen or Agnes or Richard Gilder
words: I was led up. I figured I'm getting to work about 8 or 9. And I quit at 6 or some place along there. So I wouldn't be too growly on Monday morning. I made it alright.

speaker: Glen or Agnes or Richard Gilder
words: When I got through and retired, Social Security sent me a list of every penny I'd made all my life where it was supposed to be. But it was what I'd made since they passed the Social Security law.

speaker: Glen or Agnes or Richard Gilder
words: So I looked at that and then I, one day I was just studying around here and I added up the value of the place and the value of the equipment on it and the stock on it. I had it all up and according to Social Security, I had never spent a cent in my life. I had just about over and above what they knew about it than I had made.

speaker: Glen or Agnes or Richard Gilder
words: So I guess they think I'm pretty much a tight one.

speaker: Glen or Agnes or Richard Gilder
words: I guess your work was yielding some pretty good dividends that you never collected. Yeah, I think that's probably it. No, I'd have to plow back. I did plow back. If we could get a few dollars, we'd make some improvement. Maybe clear some land or get some stock or maybe this or that or those things.

speaker: Glen or Agnes or Richard Gilder
words: But you had to plow everything that you made. I plowed back everything above our living and taxes and plowed back into place.

speaker: Glen or Agnes or Richard Gilder
words: You couldn't see where you were making a dog -gone bed of head where you think you're going behind, but I don't believe I was.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think there's a real difference?

speaker: Glen or Agnes or Richard Gilder
words: Like we're talking about some of these people in potlatch couldn't get ahead. Others seem to save it up and keep saving it. Or even today when I think about some people that make a lot of money, never have any. Never managed to hold on to any.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Is there something to do with thrift?

speaker: Glen or Agnes or Richard Gilder
words: Some people can just get what they need and others' needs are so much greater.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What do they call that? Keeping up with the Joneses?

speaker: Glen or Agnes or Richard Gilder
words: That was every place. They live at the very extreme of their means and sometimes, most of the time, beyond it. There were others that tried to make a little headway every day. That would be guys that after the mill was down for six months or a year could still pay their bill.

speaker: Glen or Agnes or Richard Gilder
words: Yeah, there were all types of humanity in potlatch and flus and every place else around there. Well, I think that's true today. People that drive have two cars, one of them is a Continental and the other is another one is a Big House. And when they're either laid off or for other reasons have to stop working, they have nothing at all. Simply because all the time they were trying to make a show of wealth and actually had their little tree of more parents than real. But it didn't only happen in potlatch, it happened every place. When Boeing shut down in Seattle over there, we know several instances of people that had Nobel Jobs. They were right up at the top.

speaker: Glen or Agnes or Richard Gilder
words: They shut that plant down and it just wasn't only a matter of days until they were desperate. Well, it's true today in Detroit too. Most people heard them, they were really unhappy because they had nothing at all. You read about or see on TV how some of them have gone to Florida and won the great last plane. Well, that'll be it because when they get back, they'll be living on welfare but then the welfare's gone.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What about the early days though?

speaker: Glen or Agnes or Richard Gilder
words: Like when you were a kid and you had the family farm, your father did.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did he see in the future that he'd be able to get out in daylight and have the place paid off and everything going well? Could he really see the light at the end of the tunnel or was he just holding on and trying to get by?

speaker: Glen or Agnes or Richard Gilder

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think most of the neighbors and people around there were, the farmers were real careful about the saving and trying to get ahead?

But they did try to know that there's going to be play food and everything that they need to live has got to be there where they can put their finger on it as they need it. It was a must and they did it. Garden stock, milk, cream, butter eggs, chickens, flour, all came in in the scheme of things and they were planned from year to year and worked out as a schedule. They had to.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What about the kids?

speaker: Glen or Agnes or Richard Gilder
words: Like, you start working out.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you get to keep the money that you made when you worked out or did it go to the family?

speaker: Glen or Agnes or Richard Gilder
words: They never asked me to give them a dime. But damn it, if they needed anything, there was some bill coming up that was going to be hard to pay. Money didn't mean anything to us. Here, Dad. We all did that. Everybody did.

speaker: Glen or Agnes or Richard Gilder
words: We'd buy our own clothes. That's the first thing. Get all of our school clothes and all the clothes we'd need for the whole school year. And after that, if we had a dollar or two a week, that's all we needed. A dollar a week was plenty.

speaker: Glen or Agnes or Richard Gilder
words: It was not uncommon for the boys to help Dad all over the neighborhood, every place. See, everything was... Well, they were part of the machine, just the same as any other member of the family. They considered themselves that way and the rest of the family considered it that way.

speaker: Glen or Agnes or Richard Gilder
words: There was a frugal. There was no trying to splurge or put on a big front. You just put on the front that you had.

speaker: Glen or Agnes or Richard Gilder
words: All through that farming country, there or here or anyplace else. Everybody was on the same plane. There was no distinction.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What do you mean? That everybody was more or less about the same, well off? Or that even if people weren't the same, it didn't matter?

speaker: Glen or Agnes or Richard Gilder
words: No, not in that way.

speaker: Glen or Agnes or Richard Gilder
words: Do you think that there was a competition as far as... Well, imagine everybody would want to get ahead as much as he could, I mean, get as well off as he could. But that wasn't a matter of competing with... just a self -advancement. No, there was no competition there, no. It was up to you to pull yourself. Nobody tried to discourage you or hold you back. They were darn sight more apt to go and help you.

speaker: Glen or Agnes or Richard Gilder
words: No. No. There was no competition between the farmers because they sold the produce on a common market to the stockyards and the warehouses. No, there was nothing to compete for.

speaker: Glen or Agnes or Richard Gilder
words: I wouldn't imagine it was ever at the expense of another person. No. No. No. A man knew better than to make it at the expense of another person because he had to live in that neighborhood and he knew what the neighborhood's opinion was going to be if he didn't behave himself.

speaker: Glen or Agnes or Richard Gilder
words: I think probably the way I look at it, the basic thing that they had, they had the property, they had land. They gave them a degree of security. And they didn't have to feel that they had to cheat or to buy or to steal because they simply had the security of their own property. And that gave them a certain dignity and that was the reason they were all good enough to be able to do that. Well, if a man would lie or steal in the first 50 years of my life, by golly, you know, he was just automatically kind of put down. He was kind of ostracized against a little bit. He didn't have the confidence of the whole neighborhood. That's what I mean. He'd be like an enemy in your group. He'd be ostracized. But they didn't. They didn't. They wouldn't lie to you. Damn it, a man's word was his bond and that's all the bond he had.

speaker: Glen or Agnes or Richard Gilder
words: Sometimes I think it must have been a far more civilized period of time than we're faced with today. They were civil people. I'm sure of it.

speaker: Glen or Agnes or Richard Gilder
words: A man was crooked.

speaker: Glen or Agnes or Richard Gilder
words: The whole neighborhood knew he was crooked and treated him as such.

speaker: Glen or Agnes or Richard Gilder
words: And sometimes they'd go to the whole extent where it was the whole county.

speaker: Glen or Agnes or Richard Gilder
words: If he crooked, if the word was no good, people knew that they didn't have much in that man to invest in. Yeah.

speaker: Glen or Agnes or Richard Gilder
words: Well, confidence was the only thing that you had to deal on. Good, reliable, stable confidence was the biggest thing you could have.

words: I don't know just how to get a comparison there, but you know, when we were young, if we made three dollars a day, we were making good money. And we had all the things that we needed. speaker: Glen or Agnes or Richard Gilder
words: There's a lot of difference between need and want. Now, those two kinds of wants, too. That's actually wanting something that's necessary and wanting that, well, pick off the top of the rainbow.

speaker: Glen or Agnes or Richard Gilder
words: It's a basic one, what I mean.

speaker: Glen or Agnes or Richard Gilder
words: You know, when, oh, up until 1930, a new family moved into the neighborhood, and the neighbors would watch them closely. And if they thought they needed help, I've known them to just make a great big neighborhood party up at them people's places, and everybody take them a present. Maybe a sack of flour, or maybe a sack of spuds, or maybe a ham, or a strip of bacon, or a quilt the old lady made. And, God, I can just tell you all kinds of parties that they had of that kind. If a house burned down, inside of a week, they had a new one. And the carpenter didn't cost him a penny.

speaker: Glen or Agnes or Richard Gilder
words: One time there at Harvard, there was an Elmer Roberts house burned down. That's on that, I don't know who owns it now, it used to be all the Chase, and then Roberts, and then Gao. But that house burned down. They took up a collection to buy windows and nails and hinges. This was a neighborhood collection. And they sent somebody to Potlatch to get that the next day. They sent everybody that could spare a dollar or two, or had a few logs. They go out and cut these logs, and everybody pitching, they had a team, maybe they could own a haul of three or four logs, or maybe a haul of seven or eight, but they took them to a mill. And the miller didn't charge them a cent to saw that up into lumber. And as soon as they saw that into lumber, the farmer's right there, pulled it back. And from the tree to that house, that Roberts house is still standing up there, and that lumber wasn't any of it, a week old when it was lived in.

speaker: Glen or Agnes or Richard Gilder
words: They just fixed him up good. And it wasn't only there, it was up at Ray Smith's, it was over at Matt Berger's, it was...

speaker: Glen or Agnes or Richard Gilder
words: They not only built the house, they logged the logs and sawed it into lumber and furnished the hinges and nails and window glass.

speaker: Glen or Agnes or Richard Gilder
words: It didn't cost anybody only a dollar or two. In a day or two's work.

speaker: Glen or Agnes or Richard Gilder
words: And the family didn't feel like it was charity. It wasn't. It wasn't. It was an obligation.

speaker: Glen or Agnes or Richard Gilder

Maybe they didn't talk about democracy the way people do now, but maybe it meant more. It meant more, but the number was mentioned in that way. It was... I don't know. Democratic, yes, people were Democratic 100%.

But anyway, one girl married and no two girls married. But the boys, none of them did. They just got their cabin back there and lived there. They tend to their own business strictly. If somebody needed help, my dad used to send me over there numerous times and want Bill or Charlie or some of them to come over and help him a day or two. They would come willingly, but there was this much that they would do. They brought their own lunch. They didn't go to the house. They didn't go in the house. When quitting time come, they just took off over the hill and that was all there was to it. But actually,

I lived there from 1934 to almost 1940 and I never saw a thing of them or heard a thing and they never bothered in any way. Never in any way. Never. If they were bothering me, if they were bothering her, it was because they... They never bothered her. I know they didn't, but he thought they did. Maybe because they ignored her. Maybe that's what they ignored her for. No, they tended to their own business.

speaker: Glen or Agnes or Richard Gilder

speaker: Glen or Agnes or Richard Gilder
