speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, there were just a bunch of - Well, it's been almost 50 years, so how did you feel about it after that time?

speaker: Glen or Agnes or Richard Gilder
words: I really wouldn't care too much. I mean, I would be kind of resentful in fact. I would, I would not care. Well, I'm surprised that such a big effort was made. Was he an especially popular person or is that just what they do if anybody would - Oh, that's what they do with anybody because Cleveno was lost up there just a few years later and they did the same thing.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: That was from Genesee, right?

speaker: Glen or Agnes or Richard Gilder
words: Yeah. They did the same thing. They went out there and it was snowing, blowing, and raining and cold. Hundreds on hundreds of them just spent days out there.

speaker: Glen or Agnes or Richard Gilder
words: And then they got just as much satisfaction out of that trip as they did at Harrison. They never found him or any trace of him. Well, I figured he found his gold and - They figured here just the last few years that they did, but during the time they were making a search they did not turn up a single thing. No, of course not. Well, then just in the last few years there was another man.

speaker: Glen or Agnes or Richard Gilder
words: He had - I was an old settler around Palouse and he had lived in Moscow the last few years. He disappeared. And the same thing, they hunted and hunted and hunted for him. Well, then the next year there was somebody from Wallace who disappeared at Clarkie. And the same thing, they turned out by the Talagans. They found him in a couple months though, or - They found him the next spring up there on Mallory Creek. And he had walked a long way. And this other, this Clavinow had walked a long way too. He was found - Ten miles, Farnsworth walked fifteen. His name wasn't Farnsworth. No, it wasn't Farnsworth. It was Jody McCown's father -in -law.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was there any method for the searching when they searched for these people in the old days?

speaker: Glen or Agnes or Richard Gilder
words: Yeah. They had crew leaders and they'd take a trail. And they'd walk up to the top of the mountain and then they'd space out about every eight feet. And every other man, one man go this way and the alternate man go this way.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Opposite directions?

speaker: Glen or Agnes or Richard Gilder
words: And they'd search that country by the Beauvoir Bayle inch. Little kid lost out here. Not too far away from here, I don't know. Over at White Pine. And they did that and they walked right by him once and one guy, something was in the side of his eye or something, he looked around and it was the right time they found him. He'd fallen off the stump and shot himself as he fell or something. It was just a heavy weight. Well, now they have search and rescue. They do the same thing though. And boy did they have a ball. I think they kind of made jam with him or something because they didn't have a job. Although they came around with it. They had no way to take it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did, when you were young, did they used to do that too?

speaker: Glen or Agnes or Richard Gilder
words: I mean, did your neighbors go up and... Two or three families go together. Yeah. Oh, we just had a big time.

speaker: Glen or Agnes or Richard Gilder
words: We still do it when we can. We always get some of the neighbors to go with us.

speaker: Glen or Agnes or Richard Gilder
words: Frank Herzog and his brother -in -law, two of his brother -in -laws. And his brother, I and my dad and my brother, we drove a team. Each had a team. There were three teams. And they drove in there at the head of the Palouse River and then we took a trail that went right up on top of Baldy, about two or three miles. And we'd step there one night. And the second night, my dad carried two of them old square oil cans. Ten gallons, five gallons on each side of them and us boys had all the buckets we could carry. We were just little fellas. Come down off there with, geez, we had berries. And it was just customary. One year, the Guntins, the Malcoms, the Bergers and my folks, they all went in the group.

speaker: Glen or Agnes or Richard Gilder
words: And they had a time up there, I'm telling you.

speaker: Glen or Agnes or Richard Gilder
words: We had a 22.

speaker: Glen or Agnes or Richard Gilder
words: Somebody had shot enough pheasants for supper. And they just tied them on this campfire, frying out good, and the game warden, Eric Harris, came along. Well, some of them were just frantic, you know, that that was just really going to get pinched. And finally, my dad says, come on ahead and have supper. He said, come in and have supper, never said a word. Chicken to him.

speaker: Glen or Agnes or Richard Gilder
words: Fire warden was the game warden and the deputy sheriff in those days. He just rode into camp on his old rowing horse. I can see him coming.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And they were sure, what do we do, what do we do, what do we do?

speaker: Glen or Agnes or Richard Gilder
words: Dad says they're caught, no getting away from it. He says, come on ahead and have supper. He did.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did your dad know him pretty well?

speaker: Glen or Agnes or Richard Gilder
words: Oh yeah, he just lived two or three miles from us. Yeah, we knew him real well. In fact, it's a matter of everybody in the party knew him. But you know, because he was a game warden, we had illegal game.

speaker: Glen or Agnes or Richard Gilder
words: He never said a word. He was a good old sport.

speaker: Glen or Agnes or Richard Gilder
words: But I think he appreciated that supper. He was still 12 miles from home and he was right in the shanks of the evening. He was going to get home in plum dark and if he didn't get supper, he was going to be damn hungry.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was that story you told me about that mule trade over near Kennedy Ford?

speaker: Glen or Agnes or Richard Gilder
words: It was a really good story you told me. I don't remember all the details. There was this guy who made a mule trade with somebody else.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did he trade him that blind mule?

speaker: Glen or Agnes or Richard Gilder
words: Yeah. What happened down here is just south of Linville, but he lives in Palouse now.

speaker: Glen or Agnes or Richard Gilder
words: Let's see, Sam was coming up out of the canyon with six heads, two and two. And he met somebody right at the top, a neighbor that lived right at the top of the grade going down. And the neighbor had a mule that looked like one that was in Sam's swing. They make almost identical mates, so he wanted him. And Sam says, no, you don't want him. He doesn't look good.

speaker: Glen or Agnes or Richard Gilder
words: Oh, he looked all right to me. He kept arguing and convinced Sam that he really wanted him. And Sam, as far as he was concerned, was convinced that he didn't want him. So he traded with him.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And a couple days later they met again and he says to Sam, why didn't you tell me that mule was blind?

speaker: Glen or Agnes or Richard Gilder
words: Sam says, I did. I told you he didn't look good.

speaker: Glen or Agnes or Richard Gilder
words: That was the end of it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you tell me that the telephone there by the, who was it that had the only telephone in the country? In that area? By Kennedy Ford? Was that at the Grange, the telephone? Or was it one of the farmers?

speaker: Glen or Agnes or Richard Gilder
words: Because there was that place that you went to make a phone call. Oh, Etheridge's and all the phones were pretty general, but we had been to the warehouse and my grandmother called the warehouse and wanted us to tell Mrs. Etheridge something. I don't remember what. But my uncle didn't care who it was. If we could get a joke on him, he got a kick out of it. So just before we got to Etheridge's, he took a chew of tobacco.

speaker: Glen or Agnes or Richard Gilder
words: Well, I was a big shot about ten years old. I figured I had to have a chew too, so I got my chew and I just got it good and juicy. She come up to Mrs. Etheridge's gate and he said, now you go in and tell her what Grandma said. So I strutted off up the walk, went up and knocked on the door and she comes to the door. I held my mouth full of tobacco juice. I couldn't say a word. Rushed back off the porch and spit and went back and gave her the message and took off down the sidewalk.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were you embarrassed to have to do that?

speaker: Glen or Agnes or Richard Gilder
words: No, you couldn't embarrass me. I wasn't smart enough. Well, I'm sorry.

speaker: Glen or Agnes or Richard Gilder
words: Will's dog is over here. Will or what are you calling Dave's dog is here. I guess it's there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How did the two of you meet? Us?

speaker: Glen or Agnes or Richard Gilder
words: Yeah. Grange, I guess. Yeah. In the county called Grange Hall.

speaker: Glen or Agnes or Richard Gilder
words: That's where most people met every Friday night.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How have you been since Grange?

speaker: Glen or Agnes or Richard Gilder
words: I don't know. Grange Hall would be so full of people you couldn't hardly dance. There's men just crowds in there. Two bits of ticket.

speaker: Agnes Gilder
words: Two bits a ticket?

speaker: Glen or Agnes or Richard Gilder
words: Either that or it was completely free. Yeah, well we had Grange.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What time period was this when you two met? Pardon? When was it that you met at the Grange? What time period was this?

speaker: Glen or Agnes or Richard Gilder
words: I don't know about 20's. Yeah, early 20's.

speaker: Glen or Agnes or Richard Gilder
words: About 21 or 22. Somewhere around there. I guess.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Wasn't it?

speaker: Glen or Agnes or Richard Gilder
words: Yeah, it doesn't have to be long and narrow. I don't remember exactly.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you know each other for very long before you started courting?

speaker: Glen or Agnes or Richard Gilder
words: Yeah. About, oh, 3 or 4 years. We started going together in about 26.

speaker: Glen or Agnes or Richard Gilder
words: Yeah. Yeah, she went with me two years before we got married.

speaker: Glen or Agnes or Richard Gilder
words: Just about anyway.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was it like then to go together?

speaker: Glen or Agnes or Richard Gilder
words: What did you do for... We went to dancing. Mostly. That was about all the shows. They had the picture show there and the flutes and potwax. Dances, parties, picnics, Grange. We didn't miss anything.

speaker: Glen or Agnes or Richard Gilder
words: Sounds like there was really a lot to do. Well, not much in the week time, but weekends. Pretty well filled with something or other. Yeah, that'd be something. It felt like it is now.

speaker: Glen or Agnes or Richard Gilder
words: Well, we just had to, more or less, the whole neighborhood created their own amusement. Grange, dances, parties, picnics.

speaker: Glen or Agnes or Richard Gilder
words: You know, they used to, in horse days, they'd drive sled loads of people from Kennedy Ford up to Harvard to Grange and drive from Harvard down to Kennedy Ford to Grange, or dances. Spend the whole day going and more than half a night getting home again.

speaker: Glen or Agnes or Richard Gilder
words: 30 below zero, we were just comfortable and had just as good a time as if the sun was shining. Wrapped up pretty good. They put a lantern inside and kind of blankets over the soap. And then going to great big parties. They had 16 foot California racks. There was 8 inch sides and then they put 12, 14 inch sideboards above that and put in about that much fresh hay. A few inches. And then put a big tarp over it. And then they'd have a lot of these lap robes and blankets and such and such. Put them all over so then it jambos tarp over the whole thing. And they just sat in there and had a party going and coming.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How many people could ride in the back of one of those?

speaker: Glen or Agnes or Richard Gilder
words: Oh, 15, 16. Depends on how much we wanted to get crowded. Us kids used to like to have them crowded pretty good, you know. Take a girl and go.

speaker: Agnes Gilder
words: Who was that?

speaker: Glen or Agnes or Richard Gilder
words: The older folks would get mad at us. He was coming to Kennedy Ford, I'm pointing you out. He was going from Harvard to Kennedy Ford. That was before he and I started going to the other side.

speaker: Glen or Agnes or Richard Gilder
words: Anyways, that's why we tried to find out who the girl was.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did many of the young folks that got married meet there like that?

speaker: Glen or Agnes or Richard Gilder
words: Sure.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Where else was there?

speaker: Glen or Agnes or Richard Gilder
words: Well, a lot of people married just locally then. Just riding in the area and didn't go that far. No, they were mostly locals. There were cars but in the wintertime nobody used them. They didn't have roads to use them on. No, they didn't dig them out like they do now.

speaker: Glen or Agnes or Richard Gilder
words: But I mean a lot of the young people just got married right there. You didn't have to go to Moscow or Spokane to meet the girl. No, not at all. Some of them did. Yeah, of course. I was thinking here lately there's been several around but the same old way. They just either go to church at the same place or a school or something like that. They're doing about the same as they used to, marrying close to home as they ever did. I've read about it to say that there's more marriages made in the same town than there are ever from away. Of course, now that the boys are in the army they do meet more girls but it's a really... ...more from the same, almost the same address.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did they get married pretty young? A lot of them? Or was it about the same as now you think?

speaker: Glen or Agnes or Richard Gilder
words: Yeah, lots of them got married real young. I was 26 I think, 27. She was two years younger. 26 when you got married. I was 25.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were you pretty concerned then, Glenn, about having enough to set up a household when you got married? Was that a big concern?

speaker: Glen or Agnes or Richard Gilder
words: Oh no. I was batching while we got married.

speaker: Glen or Agnes or Richard Gilder
words: Frank Kilmer was going to leave the neighborhood and going down to Bend, Oregon I think. And he said to Harold, he says, Harold, I want to sell this furniture, the whole houseful.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: He took out a few pieces, you know, he wanted to keep and Harold says, how much do you want for it?

speaker: Glen or Agnes or Richard Gilder
words: And he said, ten dollars. Harold come and talk to me about it and I think we each had five dollars and so we bought it. So it was no problem and then when we got married, her mother bought her a new bed, mattress and springs. And we were set up, we had everything.

speaker: Glen or Agnes or Richard Gilder
words: We had four old skate horses and two or three cows.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Four old skate? What's that?

speaker: Glen or Agnes or Richard Gilder
words: Well, it was old horses. They were, they passed their prime.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You got, with that ten dollars, you got some farm machinery too, didn't you?

speaker: Glen or Agnes or Richard Gilder
words: No. No, we got our farm machinery from Gus Klum.

speaker: Glen or Agnes or Richard Gilder
words: I think we gave him twenty -five dollars. And then I bought a sale, I bought a plow and harness and we bought these horses as a sale. Bought one of the cows over at Glen Emery's sale. I don't know where we got the other one.

speaker: Glen or Agnes or Richard Gilder
words: But we were on the road and then after I got married, you know my brother and I used to keep track of every damn penny we spent and we put it in the book. So I got married and then he was going to get married so he made a deal to buy a little place up there right by the Menin Hall Cemetery. Doesn't even show there anymore.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And he says, who's going to buy who out?

speaker: Glen or Agnes or Richard Gilder
words: And I said, well it's here, let me buy you out. Okay. We took the book and added up and divided it in half and the whole deal took about ten minutes and it didn't involve a hundred dollars.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So you bought the outfit that you'd been sharing?

speaker: Glen or Agnes or Richard Gilder
words: Yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you think you needed much to set up housekeeping, Agnes, in those days? I mean when you got married were you looking out for much?

speaker: Glen or Agnes or Richard Gilder
words: Well, they gave me a shower and a few wedding gifts so it was really all I needed so I think soon afterward he bought that desk over there.

speaker: Glen or Agnes or Richard Gilder
words: And we had a few chairs and while we had that outfit that he was telling you about it wasn't enough.

speaker: Glen or Agnes or Richard Gilder
words: It could have been more than it was enough to get by so we did. It wasn't too fancy. You know I got a kick out of it. I mean you had it now though. I got a kick out of this deal this spring. We got married, we had a certain deal and Rosemary got married and she had an entirely different deal. We went to the judge and got married. It took about five minutes.

speaker: Glen or Agnes or Richard Gilder
words: We made a date the night before that whenever I was to get off work at three o 'clock and she was going to meet me over at the mill.

speaker: Glen or Agnes or Richard Gilder
words: So she met me at three o 'clock and we never told her folks, my folks or nobody else. We just drove over to Moscow to adjust the space and got married. You were with Judge Nelson.

speaker: Glen or Agnes or Richard Gilder
words: Come home at... That's my father. We came back and did our chores and had supper and went over to her folks and told them that we got married. I made her mother mad.

speaker: Glen or Agnes or Richard Gilder
words: Because we'd do it that way just under our principles.

speaker: Glen or Agnes or Richard Gilder
words: I thought she never would make up for us but she did. A couple of days. Well she was okay. She got over it. A couple of days.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you want to get married without telling them because you were worried that they'd want to make a big deal out of it?

speaker: Glen or Agnes or Richard Gilder
words: No. No, we just didn't give a hoop. We wanted to get married and we went over and got married. And boy, the next night we got shivaried.

speaker: Glen or Agnes or Richard Gilder
words: We had a house that didn't have any studding in it. It was not a frame house. They called them a box. They just took... the inside wall and the outside wall looked just like that. They had a two by four at the top and one at the bottom. And then two inch air space between. And we seen these cars coming in and we knew what was happening so we went outside. And the other guy came up there and started shivering in us. And Ray Hansen and my brother, I guess one of the Walker boys, got an old post and went banging on that wall. Hell, they'd shake it that far in and out. And I had just got through milking and carried a three gallon bucket of milk in and settled on the drain board by the sink. And there was another two gallon bucket of water sitting on that. Well, they was right behind that and they kicked that off on the floor. God, about that time we thought we'd better go inside. And there was that water an inch deep all over the floor and that rod just come right on in and didn't pay any attention to it.

speaker: Glen or Agnes or Richard Gilder
words: They swept it out a little, mopped it out a little.

speaker: Glen or Agnes or Richard Gilder
words: But the next morning when she went to scrub the floor where that milk had began to sour, I guess she had quite a job.

speaker: Glen or Agnes or Richard Gilder
words: Yeah, most of it out the first night, I think. Yeah, it swept all it could.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So after they came in, what did they do, dance or what? Was it just a party?

speaker: Glen or Agnes or Richard Gilder
words: Just a little, oh, you're supposed to give them candy and cookies or whatever you had. And, oh, gum, I don't know, they didn't ask for beer or anything like that. But they used to in the old days, they had to have beer. No, they didn't have anything to drink at night. We had candy in the night, we didn't make coffee or anything. You were ready for them. Yeah, they had all the cigars and the kids. But, yeah, we had to be ready for them. They really would tear up any life, I guess.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You had that, you had a little place ready to move into then?

speaker: Glen or Agnes or Richard Gilder
words: Oh yeah, oh yeah, I'd been living on that place, batching for a couple of years.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What did you do?

speaker: Glen or Agnes or Richard Gilder
words: Tired of my own cooking. In fact, I got tired of beans and fried eggs. I don't know how you know what is.

speaker: Glen or Agnes or Richard Gilder
words: Well, Harold was a pretty good cook, better than I was at that time anyway. Well, we didn't have time to cook.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Your brothers?

speaker: Glen or Agnes or Richard Gilder
words: Yeah. The winter time wasn't too bad.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You were working that hard, you didn't have any time?

speaker: Glen or Agnes or Richard Gilder
words: We were both working at the mill. For me.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You hadn't had that much cooking experience before?

speaker: Glen or Agnes or Richard Gilder
words: I hadn't had a great deal. My mother thought she could do it better, so she wanted to do it. I didn't know how to cook meat or make pies or biscuits. I had to learn all that back when I could boil potatoes, I guess.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, she wasn't worried about teaching you those home arts so that you'd be able to catch yourself a handsome man?

speaker: Glen or Agnes or Richard Gilder
words: No, what she liked to do, she did. She liked to make pie. She didn't like to make cake, so I'd make cake, she made pie. And she liked biscuits, so she'd make biscuits. So I had sort of learned, of course I knew how to do it because it washed her enough, but still I had to learn, had a lot to learn, that's for sure. So I learned it in a hurry. She only had two fizzles on her biscuits. One of them didn't rise, only about that much, and the other one rose up pretty darn nice and she burned it. Oh, I don't remember those. You forgot them.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think your mother was all together surprised when you came back married or did you think she was expecting?

speaker: Glen or Agnes or Richard Gilder
words: Oh, I don't think she was too surprised. I don't think she liked it very well, but I think she had an idea what was going on. She didn't want to lose a daughter and she didn't particularly want to get another son.

speaker: Glen or Agnes or Richard Gilder
words: She worked hard and needed help. She was about 68 years old, I think.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Really?

speaker: Glen or Agnes or Richard Gilder
words: She was 44 when I was born, so it was about time she needed help, but I got out and that kind of didn't sound right. Well, I thought most mothers were so concerned about marrying their daughters off before they got to be old maids.

speaker: Glen or Agnes or Richard Gilder
words: They used to claim that, but I don't know if it was really true. Oh.

speaker: Glen or Agnes or Richard Gilder
words: Well, she worked hard there. She, well, she'd milk half the cows they had and that would be from 15 to 20, I think, the way I remember it. And she'd milk her half and I helped for the rest of it. So when she had to do it all, it was a little bit too much work. I see. It would have been for me, you know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You were the only one left at home?

speaker: Glen or Agnes or Richard Gilder
words: Well, my brother was there, but I never did milk.

speaker: Glen or Agnes or Richard Gilder
words: They had bought a milking machine at that time, but they thought they had to go along the strip afterwards.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Go along the what?

speaker: Glen or Agnes or Richard Gilder
words: Milk after the machine. Oh. Well, you had to strip after those old first models.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Because they didn't do that good a job, did they?

speaker: Glen or Agnes or Richard Gilder
words: No, they didn't.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Would you have to work most of the day to do the work you had to do around the house?

speaker: Glen or Agnes or Richard Gilder
words: Oh, about half a day in the morning, or you know, the morning, and a couple hours at night. Or, you know, night, or take care of the night milking.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were they doing, were you doing canning and all that stuff when you, back then, like you do now?

speaker: Glen or Agnes or Richard Gilder
words: Sure. Sure.

speaker: Glen or Agnes or Richard Gilder
words: I can't remember, my mother canny when I was a child, but in later years, she canned everything she could get her hands on. She had lived in North Dakota, and that was the worst place for her, that she, for she didn't have a roof. So when she came west to the coast, she canned everything she saw, and a lot of stuff that really wasn't worth it, but she thought she had to can it, you know. It was kind of a compulsion with her.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And, uh, Cattle?

speaker: Glen or Agnes or Richard Gilder
words: Yeah, we'd boil the fruit and the sugar, and if we wanted water, we'd make this, or can it in the kettle, and then dip it out of the kettle into the jars, and seal it, and then turn them upside down. Uh, along about 1930 or 35, I started in canning like we do now, and put them in the jars, and then, uh, water, or, poured the water over them, and, uh, boiled them in the canner. And they called it a coal packing hen. And then we got a pressure cooker after a while. Well, I never pressure cooked fruit. It was all just vegetables. I didn't pressure cook. But you learned to can from just watching her do it when you were growing up. Yeah, I'm reading what I didn't understand about her reading. It isn't hard. I mean, it was hard work, but I mean, it was hard to can. There was nothing complicated about it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did she use a lot of the old home remedies that you learned about when you were growing up?

speaker: Glen or Agnes or Richard Gilder
words: Not a great deal. So, uh, no. She had some cough syrup she made that I used to love. I used to like to have a cold. So, uh, it seemed like she used, uh, honey and,

speaker: Glen or Agnes or Richard Gilder
words: honey and butter, and something else. I don't know what it was, but anyway, it tasted awful good.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: They boil it up together and, oh, what the hell are you doing?

speaker: Glen or Agnes or Richard Gilder
words: My dad used to take these buds, and they called them bongilian buds. Well, he bought that, though. He bought it and then would, uh, put some alcohol with it some way. And we used to take that per cough syrup and put it on sugar, a spoonful of sugar, and, uh... Never got high. Well, we used to like to have a cold for that, too. Well, we used to, uh... A drop or two of it. Well, along about that time, we still used mustard plasters and kerosene and lard and a little. It was kind of going out, but then we still used it. Nope, that's something that goes to never, uh, in fact, I never, never did use a mustard plaster. I don't think I used it on my own kids, either.

speaker: Glen or Agnes or Richard Gilder
words: Well, I got my syrup and plastered on me. By the time we catch cold, that's what we get. Oh, the vapor rubbed the... we were using vapor rubbers. Our kids were little, and we put that...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I think it would reduce the number of colds you got, didn't it? Hmm?

speaker: Glen or Agnes or Richard Gilder
words: Didn't want to get a cold very often. No, that's, that's trying to shut it off. We didn't get a cold. We plastered on the chest with fried onions. Yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: In a, in a poultice kind of thing?

speaker: Glen or Agnes or Richard Gilder
words: Yeah, I think you put it on a cloth and then put it on the chest. I saw a woman fix a pan of onions and put it on a cow one time. The cow had a, um, a nail or something in her gout, or stomach. I just wanted to know if that was going to let you have a cold, so she picked that up for it. Remember Mrs.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Palatier?

speaker: Glen or Agnes or Richard Gilder
words: No, no Mr. Nairny. The guy in here. Uh huh. But the cow died of, um, of wire in it. Yeah, she had wire in her heart. You got the cow from Mrs. Palatier, yeah.

speaker: Glen or Agnes or Richard Gilder
words: One time, uh, my brother got a... that was back in Wisconsin. Mom was telling me about the... He, um, he got an awful cold, and she thought he was... thought he got in the way of you real bad, so... Um, she put pancakes, hot pancakes on his chest. And, uh, she was frying them away and making them for dinner. She decided that there was no need to make more pancakes. She took the ones that were already made and warmed them up, put them on his chest, and it was rough. You know, it stopped the cold.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Really?

speaker: Glen or Agnes or Richard Gilder
words: Really.

speaker: Glen or Agnes or Richard Gilder
words: Well, we'd long ways from a doctor's rule, and tried everything under the sun before we... Well, some of those old methods were just as good as... being a doctor anyway, because that's what we used, probably. Anyway, it worked. The man's still around. Seventy years later or more.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Eight more than that?

speaker: Glen or Agnes or Richard Gilder
words: Well, no. Yeah. The doctor used to go around. Were there, um, any, uh, precautions that... that you were told, that you learned about, uh, ideas of ways to...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: to do things when you're pregnant or when you're having a kid?

speaker: Glen or Agnes or Richard Gilder
words: Mm, well, he had a lot of old, wide fields. Well, I kind of was a w... Um, before my time, I had an old doctor book here. I used to... I used to look at that dog on the doctor book and find me about the third one, and I didn't have to look at it anymore. I think the thing got thrown away, so I don't know where it is, but boy, that really had some, you know, wide fields in that. Curly hair.

speaker: Glen or Agnes or Richard Gilder
words: Were there... were there midwives when you...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: when you were young in the area?

speaker: Glen or Agnes or Richard Gilder
words: Not that I know of. My mother was, uh, about the same as the LPM, but... and she took care of babies. If the doctor didn't happen to be there in time, well, she took care of them or, you know, went through the whole deal.

speaker: Glen or Agnes or Richard Gilder
words: That would go around the coast, or, uh, well, around Seattle and then with it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What do you call it?

speaker: Glen or Agnes or Richard Gilder
words: Linden, man. We called it Cedar Valley, but, uh, it was in that area.

speaker: Glen or Agnes or Richard Gilder
words: In fact, I think the doctor was just a little bit pokey and didn't care whether he got there. He always charged us the same, but he didn't have to be there when he was there.

speaker: Glen or Agnes or Richard Gilder
words: I've heard stories like, like, uh, you had to watch out for the cats with a new baby because they... They could try to scratch their eyes out or something like that. Well, they used to say that they'd, uh, uh, take their breath away. Well, what the cat was trying to do was just to get warm, you know. The baby was warm. A cat will sit on your lap or get on your chest, you know, to keep warm. That was all they were trying to do. I don't think it ever, ever hurt. It might have hurt a very, very young baby, but, uh, I don't think it ever bothered anything. More people didn't want to get close enough to find out. Well, yeah, they, they had their heads up. Still believed it, in a way. Well, I didn't want to... I think I still do. I didn't want any cats around my kids. I wouldn't want them, I mean, if they were little either, but it wasn't on that account. I never figured it was going to bother me.

speaker: Glen or Agnes or Richard Gilder
words: Well, I suppose in a few cases they smothered them, but it wouldn't happen very often. I never saw a cat get any farther than just under a child's will on the chest.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What about this idea of a certain length of time you were supposed to do nothing after you had a baby? What was that?

speaker: Glen or Agnes or Richard Gilder
words: It was about 14 days that you were supposed to stay in bed, and then you could get up and go. I don't know, I don't know why anybody would want to stay two weeks in bed. Now to get up about the third day at the very latest, but you weren't supposed to do anything for 14 days. Somebody told me once that that was a way that the women were working so hard that that was a way to make sure they didn't overdo it at the first.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, could they not do it?

speaker: Glen or Agnes or Richard Gilder
words: Well, there was a lot in it because they had to carry water and they did all kinds of outside things. Well, at the same time they knew that the Indian women were getting up the same day and that if they could stand up the white women could have, but they wanted to really.

speaker: Glen or Agnes or Richard Gilder
words: But they told you to stay down, so most of them did. I think you'd be awfully weak when you finally got up after that. Yes, that was it. You'd feel like you had needles with pins in them. Well,

speaker: Glen or Agnes or Richard Gilder
words: I think, as near as I can remember, you never got out of bed short of 7 to 8, 10 days. Well, yeah. That was the order she got and she carried them out. When we went to the hospital, we got down to 4 or 5 days to start getting up and walk around at least. Well, that was better because you didn't have that awful feeling in your feet, so you hadn't been on for a month.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you try to be very careful when you were expecting a kid to follow whatever?

speaker: Glen or Agnes or Richard Gilder
words: Well, yeah. I tried to do what I supposed to, yeah. I never had too much trouble.

speaker: Glen or Agnes or Richard Gilder
words: Going back to that cat, or kitten getting on baby, one time I went in to see our, or see why our oldest one was real quiet. Well, she was still asleep, but there was a little mouse just curled up on her about there. I just let out a scream and she woke up. I was scared. She didn't even know it, but that little thing was curled up just like a cat does.

speaker: Glen or Agnes or Richard Gilder
words: I watched her after that, but more closely than I did for the cat.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think you brought up your kids the same way you were brought up? Or do you think you're very different?

speaker: Glen or Agnes or Richard Gilder
words: Never thought of it. Well, yeah. I think our kids were brought up different than I was because I was the youngest of our family. And I had three sisters and two brothers. And all the sisters, anyway, tried to bring me up. They brought me up. And I got so aggravated when I was a child. The whole bunch of them lied on me, you know, every time I did anything they thought was wrong or how they wanted it done, they'd tell me. So I kind of laid down a rule that one person was supposed to do the disciplining. That isn't the word, but anyway, doing the discipline. One person should do it and not the whole family, if you know what I mean. I think we pretty well held that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So that person was you and not that?

speaker: Glen or Agnes or Richard Gilder
words: No, he did his part. What I mean is, if it was real bad, why, I'd tell them about it. If it wasn't too bad and I didn't want them to yell that too much, why, I did the yelling. Maybe I did more than he did. I don't know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How about it, Richard? What do you think about that?

speaker: Glen or Agnes or Richard Gilder
words: I think it was dependent upon who was around. Well, that was right. I think that whoever was there. Another thing about bringing up kids, if you wait too long to discipline, do your discipline, you forget. Well, it didn't take effect either. It's better to do it. When you see something being done, tell them to behave themselves then, not wait till they have forgotten it and you've forgotten it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think that you were easier on the kids than your parents were on you?

speaker: Glen or Agnes or Richard Gilder
words: I wondered how these things changed through time, you know, from the early days to getting on with this younger generation. Well, as far as my family was concerned, I was the youngest of them, I said, and my dad was pretty harsh on the older ones. But my mother, I was told this after I almost know who I am now, but anyway, my sister said that, my mother said that if he spanked me like he did the older ones, that she was going to get out of there and she wouldn't get a chance to do it again. So, I only remember about one, I only remember one spanking. And then he was feeling bad and he thought differently than it was. He gave me what I figured was a spanking for nothing. And my mother, I only remember one spanking. I called her a devil. She turned me over, her knees so fast that I didn't know what was happening.

speaker: Glen or Agnes or Richard Gilder
words: Do you remember Glenn when we were talking to Mamie and Mamie Werman and Mary Lynn that they were both saying that they steered clear of their fathers pretty much at the time, that they didn't want to get in his way. They were much closer to their mothers, both of them, where the father seemed to be a little more farther away from them and stricter. Well, I think they were because the father was from daylight till dark. He was out in the field. I never seen him.

speaker: Glen or Agnes or Richard Gilder
words: Well, I thought more, I think I thought more of my dad than I did of my mother. But he was, we lived an hour's ride on a ferryboat from Seattle and he was working there and we were on a farm when I was about six to nine.

speaker: Glen or Agnes or Richard Gilder
words: And I missed him. I always thought about my dad. Never had any fear of him. And that spanking, it happened before we moved to Oahu. So, I really, I think I thought more of him than I did of my mother. Although I thought about a lot of her too. It wasn't that I didn't have any lack of love for her, but I, just that I liked him more.

speaker: Glen or Agnes or Richard Gilder
words: I was always afraid that if anything happened that I knew I wouldn't, that I could not tell a lie. I was a lot of times pretty worried that I'd have to say, or that I wanted to say a lie, but I was very scared to because I knew he'd find it out.

speaker: Glen or Agnes or Richard Gilder
words: Well, even in that case, I think it was more in connection with the other part of the, other brothers and sisters that something had happened and if he had asked me, I wouldn't want to tell him what happened. But, I knew I had to.

speaker: Glen or Agnes or Richard Gilder
words: I got around that a lot by not saying anything. Not letting him know that I knew anything about it. I didn't go and tell him I knew all about it.

speaker: Glen or Agnes or Richard Gilder
words: That way you avoided having to lie or anything else. I avoided having to ask him. Period.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think you brought up your girls very differently from the boys? Or that people did that?

speaker: Glen or Agnes or Richard Gilder
words: No. I don't think so.

speaker: Glen or Agnes or Richard Gilder
words: I'm sure I had to make them all of them and toe the line pretty good. Tried to. Didn't know if it would make it work, but most of the time I guess it could.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you have any other friends?

speaker: Glen or Agnes or Richard Gilder
words: No, the girls got out at that when the two girls were the oldest and when he needed help, when they got out and helped. Right along with the boys because Lloyd was the third one and he was about five or six years younger than the older girls. He couldn't do a great deal at first.

speaker: Agnes Gilder
words: Did they like to go out with you?

speaker: Glen or Agnes or Richard Gilder
words: Oh, they liked to go out with me and they did go out with me. If I went out with a pitchfork, I had to have a pitchfork for each of them. If I went out to do something else with it, they had their hand right in it with me. They thought they were just bigger than I was. And they all of them were that way.

speaker: Glen or Agnes or Richard Gilder
words: If I had quite the fixed expense, they all went with me. Kathleen used to go out and fix fence with him. She was very young, and she'd go out and they'd be fixing fence about a little bit.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: About what?

speaker: Glen or Agnes or Richard Gilder
words: On a day like this, invariably, she'd get soaked to the skin. Every time, they'd go out and start to pour. Well, it was one year, it was especially rainy, and my stock was bothering me. On the back end of the place, we had very poor fence to no fence.

speaker: Glen or Agnes or Richard Gilder
words: So I'd go back to her every day, rain or shine, and she'd go with me. And it just seemed like every time we'd get back there in the middle of the afternoon, it would rain and pour down. I think the rest of them were in school, probably.

speaker: Glen or Agnes or Richard Gilder
words: She's four years younger than Edgar, and she was with a younger one. I used to ride a horse back there, and she'd ride with me. But they wanted to be out there, doing whatever we were doing.

speaker: Glen or Agnes or Richard Gilder
words: Well, things have changed a lot since we were raising the loam, but in a way, it's about the same. Maybe they feed them a little different, and take care of them different.

speaker: Glen or Agnes or Richard Gilder
words: They're all healthy, husky brunch. They had appendicitis or two, and Jane had kidney trouble. But as a general thing, they were pretty, they were husky, healthy.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think it's more different now than when you were raising your kids?

speaker: Glen or Agnes or Richard Gilder
words: What I'm wondering about is how much you raised your kids the way you were raised, when you were young. I think we tried to, at any rate.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I think you're trying to get me to say what the difference is with the little kids now than when we were raising them, aren't you?

speaker: Glen or Agnes or Richard Gilder
words: No, well, I guess I'm interested in that, but I'm wondering how these things change through time. Because if you raised your kids pretty much the way you were raised, it seems like these things have changed. Maybe we were a little bit, maybe that's why the kids, I think every grandparent thinks they're, the generation with their grandchildren are a little more, the parents are a little more lenient with them. But I imagine they're about the same. They probably raised them the way they were. Like you say, it probably goes from one generation to another. If they were real strict with them, they were going to be strict with their kids. But sometimes it doesn't work that way too. Sometimes it works just the opposite. Maybe we were a little strict, I don't know. You probably didn't, figured your kids had to tell the truth just like you had to tell the truth. Well, I warned them to. I sure tried to tell them to, but sometimes I would wonder. I suppose my folks did too with me.

speaker: Glen or Agnes or Richard Gilder
words: It seems like when your kids were growing up, things were maybe not as settled as when you were growing up. I don't know whether this is what I'm saying is true. I'm just wondering. Because times have changed more, further away from the old, you know, the pioneering times. Well, I was raised in Glen, too, I think. The pioneering was just about over for my folks, anyway. They, uh,

speaker: Glen or Agnes or Richard Gilder
words: we didn't all have a great deal to do with, but we, I don't know, Macos really weren't pioneers. Maybe they were back in North Dakota, Wisconsin. But, uh, when I came along, they had, oh, things that I used when I was a young person. We didn't have electricity. We didn't have electricity until 43 here.

speaker: Glen or Agnes or Richard Gilder
words: And maybe our eyes would have been better, but otherwise I don't know if we were any better off than we were then.

speaker: Glen or Agnes or Richard Gilder
words: I wouldn't give up the electricity for, for laughs anymore. Until we had it, but we didn't get along without it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Oh, boy, at six o 'clock. I didn't know it was so late. I was gonna wonder where I am. Maybe I'll call her and tell her I'm gonna come home. See ya.
